items.title: CVE-2026-35616: Fortinet FortiClient EMS Zero-Day (CVSS 9.8) Actively Exploited, Delivers EKZ Infostealer
items.summary: A critical unauthenticated RCE zero-day (CVSS 9.8) in Fortinet FortiClient EMS 7.4.5–7.4.6 is being actively exploited in the wild. Attackers are distributing fake Fortinet patch notices that deliver a credential-stealing malware called EKZ Infostealer. watchTowr's sensors first detected exploitation on March 31, 2026, three days before Fortinet published its advisory. An emergency hotfix is available for FortiClient EMS 7.4.5 and 7.4.6.
items.why: CVSS 9.8 unauthenticated RCE in a widely-deployed enterprise endpoint management platform, with active credential-theft campaigns already underway and a zero-day exploitation window of three days before the vendor patch.

items.title: CVE-2026-2441: Chrome CSS Engine Use-After-Free Zero-Day (CVSS 8.8) Exploited in the Wild
items.summary: Google confirmed a high-severity use-after-free vulnerability (CVSS 8.8) in Chrome's CSS rendering engine was exploited in the wild before a patch was released. Attackers can trigger arbitrary code execution inside the browser sandbox by serving specially crafted HTML/CSS. The flaw was discovered by researcher Shaheen Fazim on February 11, 2026. Patched versions: Chrome 145.0.7632.75/.76 (Windows/macOS) and 144.0.7559.75 (Linux).
items.why: Zero-day browser RCE requiring no authentication — a victim only needs to visit a malicious page. Sandbox escape chaining makes this a realistic path to full system compromise at scale.

items.title: Operation Neusploit: APT28 (Fancy Bear) Weaponizes Microsoft Office CVE-2026-21509 Against Eastern European Defense Ministries
items.summary: Russian state-sponsored APT28 weaponized a Microsoft Office security-feature-bypass flaw (CVE-2026-21509, CVSS 7.8) within 72 hours of its public disclosure. In a Jan 28–30, 2026 spear-phishing blitz, 29 emails targeted defense ministries (40%), logistics operators (35%), and diplomatic entities (25%) across nine Eastern European nations including Ukraine, Slovakia, and Romania. Payloads included MiniDoor (email exfiltration RAT), PixyNetLoader, and a Covenant Grunt implant using filen.io cloud storage as C2 to blend with normal traffic.
items.why: APT28 weaponizing a critical Office flaw within 72 hours of disclosure against NATO-aligned defense and diplomatic targets demonstrates the group's continued aggressive operational tempo in the European theater.
items.source: Zscaler ThreatLabz / Trellix / The Hacker News

items.title: Iranian APT Screening Serpens Deploys 6 New RAT Variants Against US, Israel, UAE Using AppDomainManager Hijacking
items.summary: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 tracked Iranian-nexus threat actor Screening Serpens (aka Smoke Sandstorm / UNC1549) deploying six new RAT variants across two previously undocumented malware families — MiniUpdate and MiniJunk V2 — in campaigns running February through April 2026. The group uses fake job recruitment lures targeting technical professionals in the U.S., Israel, and UAE. A key new evasion technique is AppDomainManager hijacking, which manipulates .NET application initialization to disable security mechanisms before the app fully loads.
items.why: Six novel RAT variants and a sophisticated .NET evasion technique (AppDomainManager hijacking) from a state-backed Iranian group actively targeting Western defense and technology sectors during an active regional conflict.

items.title: The AI Hacking Boom: 70 Open-Source AI Offensive Security Tools Now Available, Enabling Fully Autonomous Attack Pipelines
items.summary: Hadrian's research team cataloged 70 open-source AI-powered penetration testing tools as of March 2026 — fewer than five existed before GPT-4's release in April 2023. The tools span autonomous end-to-end attack agents, AI-driven exploit generation, LLM-based binary reverse engineering, and recon platforms. Unlike human pentesters who work sequentially, these agents run attack playbooks in parallel across an entire attack surface simultaneously with no human in the loop. A real-world example: a November 2025 AI-orchestrated espionage campaign attributed to a Chinese state group autonomously handled recon, exploitation, lateral movement, and data exfiltration across 30 organizations.
items.why: The democratization of fully autonomous offensive tooling — 65+ new AI attack frameworks in 18 months — fundamentally shifts the threat landscape, lowering attacker skill requirements while dramatically increasing speed and scale of compromise.
